text.title: Core Retention Metrics and Benchmarks
text.summary: Definitions and industry benchmarks for D1/D7/D30 retention, DAU/MAU engagement ratios, and monthly churn by product type.

rule.statement: Lock every retention metric definition in a versioned SQL model (dbt or equivalent) before any reporting begins; never let an agent recompute with a slightly different filter mid-week.
rule.rationale: Definitional drift — "DAU" computed as event_count > 0 vs DISTINCT(user_id) — produces numbers that look comparable but are not, breaking trend analysis.

list.item: Dn retention = users from cohort C still active on day N / cohort C size
DAU = distinct users with qualifying event on a given calendar day
WAU = distinct users with qualifying event in a 7-day window
MAU = distinct users with qualifying event in a 28-day window
DAU/MAU ratio = stickiness; measures how often monthly users return daily
Monthly churn = users who cancel or become inactive in month / users at start of month

section.title: Retention Benchmarks by Product Type
section.p: D1 / D7 / D30 retention benchmarks. Use as reference band (min/typical/max), not a single target.

rule.statement: Suppress DAU/MAU ratio when MAU < 1000 — on tiny products it is noise, not signal.
rule.rationale: A single power user skews the ratio; the metric needs a stable denominator to be meaningful.

antipattern.description: Computing DAU from event_count > 0 instead of COUNT(DISTINCT user_id).
antipattern.reason: Multi-session users inflate DAU; the metric becomes non-comparable across time periods.

antipattern.description: Reporting D30 retention for cohorts that are only 10 days old.
antipattern.reason: Diagonal effect: newest cohorts have incomplete history; D30 comparisons are meaningless until the cohort matures to 30+ days.

antipattern.description: Comparing cohort retention across periods with different traffic mixes.
antipattern.reason: A paid-channel expansion confounds the trend; always segment by acquisition source before declaring retention improvement.
